one_line_takeaway: LogicScan mines business-logic invariants from thousands of mature on-chain contracts and uses LLM-guided contrastive auditing against target functions to catch missing protocol-level checks (F1 85.2% on DeFiHacks, 73.7% on Web3Bugs), but by design it only reasons locally within a function's call chain and therefore misses sequence-amplified, multi-step arithmetic exploits like Balancer-style rounding attacks.
key_finding: On DeFiHacks, LogicScan detects 66/70 vulnerable functions (TP=66, FP=19, FN=4), giving precision 77.6%, recall 94.3%, F1 85.2% -- the paper's headline number -- versus GPTScan's F1 35.6% (TP=16, FP=14, FN=44), ZepScope's F1 2.7%, Slither's F1 0.5%, and the LLM-Only ablation's F1 28.2% (TP=43, FP=192, FN=27). On Web3Bugs, LogicScan gets precision 66.5%, recall 82.8%, F1 73.7% (TP=111, FP=56, FN=23), versus GPTScan F1 27.3%, ZepScope F1 0.5%, Slither F1 0.0%, LLM-Only F1 26.1%. On Top200 (benign contracts, no true positives possible for any tool), LogicScan reports 18 false positives versus GPTScan's 12 (fewest), ZepScope's 48, Slither's 53, and LLM-Only's 40. Aggregated across all three datasets: LogicScan overall precision 70.2%, recall 86.8%, F1 77.7% (TP=177, FP=75, FN=27). Root-cause analysis of the 75 aggregate false positives: design-pattern deviation 38/75, distributed invariant enforcement (checks offloaded to a caller) 13/75, implicitly-encoded invariants (e.g., relying on Solidity's native overflow reverts instead of explicit require) 7/75, other 17/75. RQ2 (LLM backend swap on DeFiHacks/Web3Bugs): GPT-5 gives DeFiHacks precision/recall/F1 = 76.9/95.23/85.1, Claude-sonnet 4.5 gives 81.38/91/85.9, Qwen-3 235B gives 69.26/83.7/75.8; on Web3Bugs GPT-5 = 66.6/86.7/75.3, Claude = 64.9/84.3/73.3, Qwen-3 = 58.2/76.5/66.1 -- Claude trades recall for precision, Qwen-3 is weakest overall, but performance is broadly backend-agnostic. RQ3 (cost/runtime on DeFiHacks, 78.23 KLoc total): GPT-5 backend = 12,392s / $7.71 total (158 s/KLoc, $0.0985/KLoc); Claude Sonnet-4.5 = 9,066s / $10.00 (116 s/KLoc, $0.1280/KLoc); Qwen3 235B = 9,208s / $2.62 (120 s/KLoc, $0.0300/KLoc). RQ4 ablation (false-positive rate by dataset, Figure 8): removing both Context and Aggregation mechanisms gives FPR 12%/47%/53% on Top200/DeFiHacks/Web3Bugs; adding Multi-dimensional Code Extraction alone drops this to 10%/21.3%/38.3%; the full LogicScan (adding Noise-aware Aggregation) further drops it to 7.1%/12.8%/29% -- each mechanism contributes a measurable, monotonic FPR reduction.
core_contribution: LogicScan, an LLM-driven framework for detecting business-logic vulnerabilities in smart contracts via 'contrastive auditing': rather than writing invariant rules by hand or having an LLM infer them from scratch, LogicScan (1) mines reusable business-logic invariants from a large corpus of mature, real-world on-chain contracts, normalizing each function's precondition checks into a proposed intermediate representation called Business Specification Language (BSL) via a Generate-Validate-Repair LLM pipeline, and (2) at audit time retrieves semantically similar template functions for a target function (matched on Solidity version, call-chain semantics, and business category) and uses an LLM to check whether the target function is missing invariants that its templates consistently enforce. Two false-positive mitigation mechanisms are original to this paper's design: Multi-dimensional Code Extraction (feeding the LLM not just the call chain but also functions that write to variables used in the call chain, to fix incomplete-context false positives) and Noise-aware Logic Aggregation (a majority-voting scheme over retrieved templates' BSL checkConditions, using a manually curated domain synonym dictionary, to filter out implementation-specific or conflicting invariants before presenting a 'common BSL' to the LLM).
threat_model: Attacker exploits smart contracts (primarily DeFi protocols on Ethereum) whose implementations omit or weakly enforce a business/protocol invariant that is implicitly expected given the contract's semantic role but is not a generic coding-pattern bug (e.g., missing a health-factor check before burning collateral tokens, as in the paper's motivating Euler Finance example, a ~$197M March 2023 loss). This class is distinguished from classic bugs like reentrancy or integer overflow: it has no universal syntactic signature, is protocol/domain-specific, and is not reliably triggered by dynamic testing. The defender is an auditor/tool running LogicScan offline against a target contract's public functions, using a knowledge base mined from mature protocols as an oracle for 'what invariants are normally enforced' in a given business category (e.g., lending pools, ERC721 collections).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation: LogicScan performs local, single-function-call semantic analysis and cannot detect sequence-amplified/multi-step arithmetic bugs (its example: Balancer v2-style batchSwap precision/rounding exploits, where each individual computation's error is negligible but accumulates across many chained operations) -- this class produces false negatives because it requires reasoning across multi-step execution traces, which is outside the paper's static, local-semantics design. Internal threats to validity acknowledged by the authors: reliance on Slither for call-chain/variable-write extraction propagates any of Slither's static-analysis limitations into context completeness; residual LLM semantic errors persist despite grammar constraints and validation/repair loops. External threats acknowledged: evaluation is Ethereum/DeFi-specific, so results may not generalize to non-DeFi domains or non-EVM chains without re-adapting the logic-mining and categorization steps; future LLMs with different reasoning behavior could change results. On the Top200 benign set, all tools (including LogicScan) show zero true positives by construction (it's a false-positive-only benchmark), so LogicScan's precision/recall/F1 are undefined (marked '-') there -- the paper only reports FP counts for this dataset. Business-category labeling is a lightweight, name-token-based heuristic (not verified against ground truth), which the authors argue is tolerated by majority-vote aggregation but is not independently validated. The synonym/domain dictionary used for semantic normalization of invariant conditions is built via 'lightweight manual curation,' introducing a human-in-the-loop step not automated or evaluated for coverage/error rate. A notable false-positive case (Figure 6, self-approval NFT contract) is explicitly discussed by the authors as arguably not a true false positive from a security standpoint but rather a legitimate design deviation the tool flags as review-worthy -- illustrating that the tool's precision numbers partly reflect a conservative, deviation-flagging design choice rather than pure detection error.

datasets_used: DeFiHacks (curated from DeFiHackLabs, 52 vulnerable contracts / 70 function-level business-logic vulnerabilities, manually filtered)
Web3Bugs (Code4rena-audited projects; 65 of 81 usable after crytic-compiler parsing filter; 134 function-level vulnerabilities)
Top200 (top 200 contracts by market cap, from the GPTScan dataset, excluding proxies; treated as benign/false-positive-only)
quantitative_results: DeFiHacks: LogicScan TP=66 FP=19 FN=4, Precision 77.6%, Recall 94.3%, F1 85.2%
DeFiHacks: GPTScan TP=16 FP=14 FN=44, F1 35.6%
DeFiHacks: ZepScope TP=4 FP=217 FN=66, F1 2.7%
DeFiHacks: Slither TP=1 FP=285 FN=69, F1 0.5%
DeFiHacks: LLM-Only TP=43 FP=192 FN=27, F1 28.2%
Web3Bugs: LogicScan TP=111 FP=56 FN=23, Precision 66.5%, Recall 82.8%, F1 73.7%
Web3Bugs: GPTScan F1 27.3%, ZepScope F1 0.5%, Slither F1 0.0%, LLM-Only F1 26.1%
Top200 (benign, FP only): LogicScan FP=18, GPTScan FP=12, ZepScope FP=48, Slither FP=53, LLM-Only FP=40
Overall aggregate: LogicScan TP=177 FP=75 FN=27, Precision 70.2%, Recall 86.8%, F1 77.7%
False-positive root causes (75 total): design-pattern deviation 38, distributed invariant enforcement 13, implicitly-encoded invariants 7, other 17
RQ2 backend comparison DeFiHacks: GPT-5 76.9/95.23/85.1 (P/R/F1), Claude Sonnet-4.5 81.38/91/85.9, Qwen-3 235B 69.26/83.7/75.8
RQ2 backend comparison Web3Bugs: GPT-5 66.6/86.7/75.3, Claude Sonnet-4.5 64.9/84.3/73.3, Qwen-3 235B 58.2/76.5/66.1

RQ3 cost/runtime on 78.23 KLoc DeFiHacks corpus: GPT-5 12,392s/$7.71 (158 s/KLoc, $0.0985/KLoc); Claude Sonnet-4.5 9,066s/$10.00 (116 s/KLoc, $0.1280/KLoc); Qwen3 235B 9,208s/$2.62 (120 s/KLoc, $0.0300/KLoc)
RQ4 ablation FPR (Top200/DeFiHacks/Web3Bugs): w/o Context & Aggregation 12%/47%/53%; w/o Aggregation only 10%/21.3%/38.3%; full LogicScan 7.1%/12.8%/29%
Contract corpus: 43,749 of 817,772 Ethereum contracts retained after top-20% filtering by deployment time and transaction volume
BSL invariant corpus: >62,000 distinct extracted checkConditions, reduced to top 2,000 by frequency for the synonym dictionary
Template retrieval: top-5 candidates used (empirically >90% correct self-retrieval within top 5), similarity threshold 80%
baselines_compared: GPTScan (LLM+static-analysis SOTA tool, configured with GPT-3.5-turbo per original paper)
ZepScope (static analysis via OpenZeppelin design-pattern matching)
LLM-Only (ablation baseline using an LLM without LogicScan's retrieval/aggregation machinery)
